name: Priya (engaged grad student) — team / Parts 2 & 3
notes: Drives Remi through Parts 2 + 3 only (2.1 Planning & Execution, 2.2 Roles & Contributions, 2.3 Collaboration & Communication, 2.4 Team Health Check, 3.1 Open Question, 3.2 Commitment). Provides full roster, per-member contributions, equity reflection, five 1–5 ratings with justifications, biggest open question, and concrete commitment.
turns: Hi! I'm Priya from Team Coyote. Ready to walk through the team-process reflection.

Honestly we did NOT have a shared understanding of the week's goals at the start. Monday standup was 25 minutes and we left thinking we were aligned, but by Tuesday afternoon Marco and I were doing duplicative coding while Devi and Yusuf were already affinity-clustering. We divided the work by ownership area on Monday but didn't sequence it — that was the gap.

We did not follow our plan. We adapted by adding a 15-minute mid-week sync on Wednesday morning and writing tasks on a shared board with a single-owner rule. The change was triggered by catching the duplication on Wednesday, and in hindsight it was absolutely the right call — it cost 15 minutes and saved hours.
Nothing more on planning.
Roster: Priya (me), Marco, Devi, Yusuf — four of us total.
I drove the user interview synthesis — coding 60 of the 180 stickies and writing the first-pass cluster labels.
Marco did the bulk of the raw note transcription from the interviews and was the team's 'so what' challenger during clustering. Probably the most underrated contribution because it kept us honest.
Devi led the affinity wall physically and started the first three clusters. Strong start, but as I mentioned earlier she was moving too fast for the first 45 min — once we rebalanced it was great.
Yusuf focused on the literature side — he read the assigned synthesis readings and brought specific terminology to our debrief. He synthesized less data but raised the methodological bar.

Distribution was mostly equitable but Yusuf did less hands-on synthesis than the rest of us. If I could change one thing, I'd have asked him to drive one of the four clusters end to end so he gets the same hands-on reps.
Move on.

What worked: the Wednesday 15-minute sync I mentioned. Specifically, we ended it by each saying one sentence about what we were going to do before lunch, and that one-sentence rule killed the ambiguity that was eating Tuesday.

What broke down: Tuesday afternoon Slack thread on whether to merge two clusters. We had four people typing past each other for 40 minutes, no decision, and I think two of us walked away thinking we'd merged and two thinking we hadn't. Impact was that on Wednesday we were working from two different versions of the wall and had to reconcile.
Improvement next week: any decision that takes more than five Slack messages becomes a 5-minute synchronous call. Measurable — I can count Slack threads on Friday and check.
Move on.
Ready for the ratings.
Justification first: by midweek we had a clear shared goal but Monday we did not. Net I'd give it a 3.
Justification: everyone got airtime, but Yusuf's methodological contributions felt slightly undervalued in the moment because they didn't translate to sticky counts. 4.
Justification: the Slack thread incident proves we still resolve disagreements by attrition rather than constructively. The rest of the week was fine. 3.
Justification: we hit our internal Friday deadline for the affinity wall photo. 5.
Justification: I am confident about our synthesis but uncertain about whether our clusters point to a coherent design direction. 4.
Move on.

Our biggest open question is whether the three insight clusters we landed on — trust, recovery from errors, and self-paced exploration — actually map to one coherent user need or whether we're seeing three different user types and need to segment. This is a methods question (about how to choose between merge-vs-segment) and a problem-space question (about which user need we're really designing for).
Move on.

Next week's commitment: by Wednesday we will have either (a) a single user-need statement that absorbs all three clusters or (b) an explicit segmentation rationale that picks one cluster as primary. This is a research action with a design-decision payoff. Observable signal: the statement or rationale is written in our Notion doc and the four of us have signed off on it in writing.
I think we're done.

On the bot — yeah, this surfaced way more honest team reflection than the PDF, especially the Slack-thread incident which I would have softened in writing but admitted out loud. To make it work better next week: maybe a small 'section 4 of 6' indicator and a way to flag a teammate-name typo without restarting the section.
